reasoning: The base rate for snap elections in Serbia under the SNS is high (almost every two years), but the specific circumstances right now drastically lower the probability of an election announcement before July 1, 2025. Former PM Milos Vucevic's resignation triggered a 30-day constitutional deadline to either form a new government or call an election. To avoid early elections amidst ongoing civil protests, parliament successfully confirmed a new government on April 16, 2025, under outsider PM Djuro Macut (with a 153/250 majority). While student groups officially demanded snap elections in early May 2025, senior ruling party leaders—including Vucevic and Parliament Speaker Ana Brnabic—flatly and publicly rejected the demand on May 6–7. With a newly installed government expressly designed to bypass early elections, the ruling party's firm resistance, and waning protest momentum, there is virtually no incentive to announce an election within the 6 weeks leading up to the July 1 deadline. Even if an election occurs in late 2025 (H2), standard 45-60 day Serbian election timelines mean it would likely be announced much later in the year, well past July 1.
